title: Claude Code Gets 50% Cheaper When It Talks Like a Caveman
excerpt: A developer discovered that forcing Claude Code to respond in terse, caveman-like language cuts token output in half. It sounds absurd. The cost savings are measurable.
author: AIToolCrunch
content:
Julius Brussee built something ridiculous that actually works. His caveman GitHub repo forces Claude Code to communicate in grunts and broken speech. No flowery explanations. No chain-of-thought reasoning. Just the bare minimum: "Claude find bug. Here fix."

The repo hit Hacker News with 373 points. But the real story isn't the viral moment - it's what the data showed. Developers reported 40-50% reductions in token usage for coding sessions. For teams using Claude Code heavily, that's the difference between $50 and $100 per session.

How a skill becomes a cost optimizer

Claude has a feature called "skills" - custom instructions stored in a CLAUDE.md file that reshape how the model behaves during a session. Most developers use these for legitimate things: enforce test-driven development, prefer functional patterns, always add error handling.

Brussee's insight was simpler. Claude costs money because it talks too much.

When you ask Claude Code to fix a bug, it doesn't just fix the bug. It explains the bug. It contextualizes the bug. It walks you through the reasoning. A typical response runs 400-600 tokens of elaboration before you get the actual code change. The caveman skill murders all that extra communication.

The skill is literally seven words: "respond like a caveman from the stone age." Drop articles. Eliminate prepositions. No explanations, only action. Output shrinks to 50-100 tokens. Same information density. Fraction of the cost.

When this actually works

The caveman mode isn't universal. It fails spectacularly when you need reasoning.

Imagine debugging a race condition in concurrent code. You need Claude to think methodically. Caveman mode cuts the chain-of-thought so aggressively that the model loses its way on hard problems. You get a guess instead of analysis. That's worse than useless - it's expensive and wrong.

Where the skill shines:

  • Status updates ("done", "bug fixed", "test passes")
  • Simple refactors (renaming, restructuring, formatting)
  • Linting errors and obvious fixes
  • Code review annotations when you already know what's wrong
  • Routine task execution where the developer is in control

The pattern is clear: use caveman mode when you're in execution mode and understand the problem. Turn it off when you need Claude to reason through ambiguity.

The real cost problem nobody talks about

Claude Code pricing is nominally cheap until it isn't. A solo developer can run a week-long project for $30. But teams running autonomous coding sessions - multiple hours of continuous back-and-forth, dozens of requests per session - see bills that look wrong.

The issue is output tokens. Claude's behavior is to over-explain. That's a feature for most use cases. It reads as thoughtful. It builds trust. But for experienced developers evaluating code themselves, it's pure waste.

Most developers either don't notice this cost leak or accept it. Brussee's caveman skill is the nuclear option: stop Claude from explaining anything. But it works because the instinct is sound - for execution-mode workflows, the explanation is overhead.

Other techniques that work: keep CLAUDE.md instructions tight (every word gets re-sent as context), use the escape hatch to interrupt before Claude starts a long monologue, break large tasks into smaller sessions instead of keeping massive context windows open.

The caveman skill is bluntest. The alternatives are more nuanced. Pick based on your workflow.

The tradeoff is real

Cost reduction matters, but not if the code quality suffers. The caveman skill trades explanation for speed and cheapness. For routine tasks that trade is good. For novel problems, it's a bad deal.

The smarter approach: use caveman mode selectively. Load it for debugging sprints and refactoring sessions. Disable it for architecture work, security reviews, or any problem where you need Claude to explain its reasoning.

This is actually an argument for Claude's skill system being well-designed. You can tune the model's behavior to fit the task. Most tools force one mode. Claude lets you switch.

What this means for AI tool pricing

The caveman trick exposes something about AI pricing: the user controls more of the cost than they think. You can optimize output by changing instructions. You can batch requests to reduce context reuse. You can use cheaper models for certain tasks.

For teams managing AI tool costs, this is important. The sticker price isn't the real price. The real price depends on how you talk to the model. Optimization is possible. The caveman skill is just the most visible example.

If you're running heavy Claude Code sessions, the repo is worth testing. Add the skill to your CLAUDE.md, run a session on a task you already understand, measure the token usage. The savings are real. The vibe is genuinely strange - watching an AI respond like it was raised in a stone age hut - but for execution work, the cost math is hard to argue with.
